Links to webpages and organizations with online resources for Postdocs:

Genomics Resources for Postdocs

The National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI) of the National Institutes of Health has awarded the Molecular Sciences Institute a grant to increase the number of underrepresented minorities in the field of genomic science. (LINK TO MSI PRESS RELEASE) This grant supplements an award MSI received last year from NHGRI to support the Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation (CGEC). At that time, NIH named the CGEC one of four national Centers of Excellence in Genomic Studies. (LINK TO OTHER MSI PRESS RELEASE). The supplemental grant will allow us to increase our recruitment and training of underrepresented minorities who have received their PhD's.

Specifically, the Center for Genomic Experimentation & Computation (CGEC) seeks Underrepresented Minority Research Fellows from biology, chemistry, physics, engineering, computer science, and mathematics for work on the Alpha Project, a long-term multidisciplinary effort, directed by Professor Roger Brent, to model and predict the behavior of a G-protein receptor-coupled signal transduction pathways in yeast using a combined experimental and computational approach. Fellowships are available for both recent postdocs and mid career transitions.

The core of the CGEC is the Molecular Sciences Institute in Berkeley, California. Laboratories at four other institutions are also members of the Center. Fellows have the opportunity to work at these different sites as their research needs dictate. Positions are available in each of the following locations in the areas listed:

Molecular Sciences Institute (Berkeley, California): Roger Brent, Experimental biology-Develop ways to measure pathway output and key intermediate quantities from individual cells and populations of cells. Theory and Computation-Develop computational means to simulate the behavior of individual cells and populations of cells and build quantitative models that predict cellular responses to defined perturbations.

California Institute of Technology (Pasadena, California): Jehoshua Bruck, Division of Computation & Neural Systems & Electrical Engineering Theory and Computation-Develop computational means to simulate the behavior of individual cells and populations of cells and build quantitative models that predict cellular responses to defined perturbations. Computation and Design-Learn from biological systems about new principles in circuits for computation and communications and explore new design principles for artificial systems that compute, communicate, and evolve like biological cells.

Massachusetts Institute of Technology (Cambridge, Massachusetts): Drew Endy, Division of Biological Engineering and Department of Biology Model/Experiment Coupling-Develop and apply experimental devices and computational tools to constrain models of complex biological systems. Biological Information Theory-Develop and apply theoretical frameworks for understanding the time-dependent state of molecular systems.

Pacific Northwest National Laboratory (Richland, Washington): Richard D. Smith, Macromolecular Structure and Dynamics Mass Spectrometry-Develop and apply methods with exquisite sensitivity for the quantitative analysis of key pathway intermediates using ultra-high-pressure RP-LC coupled with ESI-FTICR and proteomic bioinformatic tools.

A fellow may choose among two options of fellowship. The first option is to immediately begin research, after receiving approval from the Center Director. The second option is to participate in a training program composed of four parts. The first component of the training program is an orientation program describing an overview of the Center and its research, including introductions to scientists in nearby institutions. The second component will come from a CGEC Rotation Program, during which the fellow will be encouraged to spend days to weeks at any of the member institutions, learning, contributing, and sharing data, to gain in situ exposure to a variety of different disciplines (computer science, mathematics, physics, engineering, chemistry and biology, etc.), and to different career environments (university departments, a national laboratory, and a multi-disciplinary research institute). Following the six-month period of introduction, the fellow will begin the third component of training in which the fellow will undertake an 18-month research project, with Center approval. In the fourth component, the fellow will participate in a series of seminars designed to enhance skills in grant writing, scientific writing, and laboratory management.

Fellows must be U.S. citizens or permanent residents. Applications should include a cover letter and CV, and 3 letters of references should be sent to:

NHGRIpostdocs, Molecular Sciences Institute

2168 Shattuck Avenue, Floor 2

Berkeley, CA 94704, USA

Or emailed to: NHGRIpostdocs@molsci.org.
